children.name: Podiceps
children.description: The large grebes — superbly adapted diving birds with lobed feet set so far back on the body that they are virtually unable to walk on land. Grebes are among the most accomplished divers of all freshwater birds, using their feet rather than wings to propel themselves underwater in pursuit of fish. The great crested grebe's elaborate courtship display — the most complex of any Swedish waterbird — involves both partners rising chest-to-chest from the water in the 'penguin dance', shaking their spectacular rufous-and-black crests and exchanging gifts of waterweed. This ceremony was first scientifically described by Julian Huxley in 1914 and remains a textbook example of ritualized courtship behaviour. The grebes' beautiful plumage made them targets for the 19th-century feather trade; their recovery catalysed early British bird protection legislation.

children.name: Podiceps cristatus
children.description: The skäggdopping — Sweden's largest grebe and one of its most beautiful birds. The elaborate head-shaking courtship dance in early spring, performed by both sexes with erect rufous-and-black crests, is one of the most spectacular sights on Swedish lakes. Nests on sheltered bays of larger lakes; chicks ride on the parents' backs beneath the wing feathers. Once hunted nearly to extinction for its silky breast plumage, which was used to trim Victorian hats; now common and increasing across lowland Sweden.

children.name: Podiceps grisegena
children.description: The gråhakedopping — a declining breeding species in southern and central Sweden, requiring larger eutrophic lakes with rich fish populations. In breeding plumage its chestnut-red neck, grey cheeks, and yellow bill base make it unmistakeable. Less showy in courtship than its great crested relative but equally devoted to its mate, with pairs duetting in loud braying calls across breeding lakes. Winters on sheltered Swedish coastal bays and the southern Baltic.

children.name: Podiceps auritus
children.description: The svarthakedopping — arguably the most stunning of all Swedish waterbirds in breeding plumage, with brilliant golden-orange ear-tufts that fan out from behind the eye like flames against a black cap. A scarce breeder in northern Sweden, particularly in Lapland where it nests on small forest pools and lochans. Winters on the open sea off the Swedish coast, where it is drably grey and white. Population trends are of conservation concern across its Scandinavian range.

children.name: Podiceps nigricollis
children.description: The world's most abundant grebe, with tens of millions staging on hypersaline lakes in western North America during migration. In breeding plumage it sports rich chestnut flanks, a glossy black neck, and striking golden-yellow ear tufts. Uniquely, it becomes almost flightless after moulting all its flight feathers simultaneously; its pectoral muscles shrink so much the bird is grounded for months each year. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Podiceps occipitalis
children.description: A small to medium-sized grebe of high-altitude Andean lakes and coastal lowlands, from Ecuador and Colombia south to Tierra del Fuego. Its silvery-white underparts and pale grey back give it a distinctive appearance among the generally darker-plumaged Andean grebes. Common on high-altitude lakes including those above 4,000 m; IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Podiceps gallardoi
children.description: One of the world's rarest grebes, named after Argentine zoologist José María Gallardo and discovered as recently as 1974. Fewer than 800 individuals remain on remote Patagonian plateau lakes; it breeds exclusively on floating mats of the aquatic plant Myriophyllum. American mink predation and kelp gull nest-robbing have caused catastrophic declines. IUCN Critically Endangered.

children.name: Podiceps major
children.description: The largest grebe in the world, reaching 77 cm in length — a powerful, long-necked diver of South American lakes, rivers, and coastal bays from Peru and Brazil south to Tierra del Fuego. Its long, sharp bill is used to catch fish through pursuit diving in deep, clear water. Despite its great size it constructs floating vegetation nests like smaller relatives. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Podiceps andinus
children.description: Extinct since 1977, last recorded on Lake Tota in the Colombian Andes — its sole known habitat. Hunting, wetland drainage, and pesticide poisoning from duck-shooting operations combined to eliminate this small high-altitude grebe. One of the most recent bird extinctions in South America; a cautionary example of the fragility of single-lake endemic species.

children.name: Tachybaptus
children.description: Little grebes — the smallest and most compact of all grebes, found on a much wider range of water bodies than their larger relatives. The little grebe (smådopping) is a remarkably buoyant bird, riding high on the water like a cork and diving suddenly with a smooth, almost silent plunge. Despite its tiny size it is feisty and territorial, driving away intruders many times its own size with a loud, shrill whinny. Little grebes are one of the most widespread wetland birds in Sweden, found on ponds as small as a few hundred square metres — anywhere there is aquatic vegetation for nesting and small fish or invertebrates to eat. In autumn they can aggregate in small flocks on larger lakes before moving to coastal wintering areas.

children.name: Tachybaptus ruficollis
children.description: The smådopping — Europe's smallest grebe, barely larger than a starling. Its rotund, tailless silhouette and habit of bouncing back to the surface after each dive make it instantly recognisable. Surprisingly widespread and common in Sweden, nesting on ponds, ditches, slow rivers, and urban park lakes. Its loud, descending whinny — a cascading trill audible across the whole wetland — is one of the characteristic sounds of a Swedish spring marsh.

children.name: Tachybaptus novaehollandiae
children.description: The most widespread grebe in Australasia, found from Indonesia and New Guinea south through Australia to New Zealand on almost any freshwater body including temporary pools in arid areas. In breeding plumage its chestnut cheek patch and yellow loral spot are diagnostic. One of the most ecologically adaptable grebes globally; quickly colonises newly formed wetlands. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Tachybaptus dominicus
children.description: The smallest grebe in the Americas, found from the southern United States and Mexico through the Caribbean south to Argentina on ponds, marshes, and slow rivers. Despite its tiny size it is boldly marked with a dark cap and throat, rufous flanks, and striking yellow eyes. Secretive; nests on tiny ponds hidden within marsh vegetation. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Tachybaptus pelzelnii
children.description: Named after Austrian ornithologist August von Pelzeln; endemic to western Madagascar's lakes and marshes. Smaller than the Little Grebe, with which it was formerly lumped; now restricted to a limited number of freshwater sites. Threatened by wetland conversion for rice cultivation, introduced fish species, and the invasive snakehead; IUCN Vulnerable.

children.name: Tachybaptus brachypterus
children.description: Known only from Buru Island in the Banda Sea, Indonesia, last reliably recorded in the early 20th century and possibly now extinct. One of the world's least-known grebes, described from only a handful of museum specimens. Its tiny island lake habitat on Buru may have been drained or fundamentally altered; IUCN Critically Endangered (possibly Extinct).

children.name: Tachybaptus tricolor
children.description: A small grebe distributed across the Indonesian archipelago, the Philippines, New Guinea, and adjacent Pacific islands. Named for its tricoloured breeding pattern of rufous neck, white cheek patch, and dark upperparts. One of the least-studied grebes in the Asia-Pacific region; inhabits lakes, marshes, and slow-flowing rivers in lowland areas. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Podilymbus
children.description: Pied-billed grebes of the Americas — stocky, thick-necked grebes with short, stout bills adapted for crushing hard-shelled prey such as crayfish and mussels. Unusual among grebes in their ability to sink silently by expelling air from their plumage, submerging like a submarine when alarmed. The Atitlán Grebe, now extinct, was a large, nearly flightless endemic of a single Guatemalan lake — lost within decades of introduced bass destabilising its ecosystem.

children.name: Podilymbus podiceps
children.description: The stockiest and most familiar grebe of the Americas, found on almost any freshwater body from Canada to southern South America. Its distinctive pied (two-coloured) bill — pale with a black band in breeding season — gives it its name. Can adjust buoyancy by changing the amount of air trapped in its plumage, allowing it to sink gradually when alarmed. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Podilymbus gigas
children.description: Extinct since 1989; once endemic to Lake Atitlán in Guatemala, where it evolved near-flightlessness over millennia of island-like isolation. Largemouth bass introduced in 1958 for sport fishing ate the grebe's food supply and young; hunting and a 1976 earthquake that lowered the lake level accelerated its decline. Last confirmed sightings were in the late 1980s.

children.name: Poliocephalus
children.description: Two small grebes of the Southern Hemisphere — the highly gregarious, nomadic Hoary-headed Grebe of Australia and the secretive, endangered New Zealand Grebe. Both are compact diving birds that lack the elaborate head plumes of Podiceps; the hoary-headed grebe's silvery streaked head is unique among grebes.

children.name: Poliocephalus poliocephalus
children.description: An Australian grebe distinguished by its extraordinary silvery-streaked head — described as 'hoary' or frost-white — contrasting with a dark body in breeding plumage. Highly nomadic and social, forming large flocks on open lakes and reservoirs across southern Australia. Unlike most grebes it is highly gregarious outside the breeding season and often found far from cover. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Poliocephalus rufopectus
children.description: A small, stocky grebe endemic to the North Island of New Zealand, known in Māori as weweia. Once widespread on the South Island too, it disappeared there before European settlement. Inhabits lakes and slow rivers with dense fringing vegetation; secretive and reluctant to fly. IUCN Nationally Endangered in New Zealand; estimated at under 2,000 individuals.

children.name: Rollandia
children.description: Two South American grebes closely associated with high-altitude lakes in the Andes — the widespread White-tufted Grebe and the Titicaca Grebe, one of the few flightless grebes in the world. The Titicaca Grebe is endemic to the world's highest navigable lake and is among the most endangered waterbirds in the Americas.

children.name: Rollandia rolland
children.description: A small, widespread grebe of South American lakes, marshes, and slow rivers from Peru and Bolivia south to Tierra del Fuego and the Falkland Islands. Named for the white ear-tufts displayed by both sexes during courtship. Adaptable to a wide range of still or slow-moving freshwater habitats from sea level to high-altitude Andean lakes. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Rollandia microptera
children.description: A large, effectively flightless grebe endemic to Lake Titicaca and a few adjacent high-altitude lakes on the Peru-Bolivia border at elevations above 3,800 m. One of the rarest grebes in the Americas; threatened by gill-net entanglement, hunting, pollution, and the introduction of rainbow trout. Its reduced wings are incapable of sustained flight. IUCN Endangered.

children.name: Aechmophorus
children.description: The western grebes of North America — two large, elegant, black-and-white grebes that are among the most spectacular of all divers. Renowned for their extraordinary 'rushing' courtship ceremony, in which paired birds sprint side-by-side across the water surface with wings swept back, reaching speeds of 20 km/h — one of the most remarkable bird displays in the world. They were once heavily hunted for their silky plumage; recovery came only after legal protection in the early 20th century.

children.name: Aechmophorus occidentalis
children.description: A spectacular black-and-white grebe of western North American lakes, famous for its dramatic rushing courtship display in which paired birds sprint across the water surface side-by-side. Feeds on fish caught through pursuit diving; one of the largest North American grebes. Nests in large colonial rookeries at the lake's edge. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Aechmophorus clarkii
children.description: Nearly identical to the Western Grebe but with white extending above the eye (black in the Western) and a brighter orange-yellow bill. Named after American explorer and boundary surveyor John Henry Clark. Long treated as a colour morph of the Western Grebe, it was confirmed as a distinct species in 1985 based on behavioural, vocal, and genetic differences. Often nests in mixed colonies with Western Grebes. IUCN Least Concern.
